skill.name: building-chatgpt-apps
skill.description: Guides developers through building ChatGPT Apps with OpenAI Apps SDK widgets, FastMCP servers, response metadata, Developer Mode setup, and common debugging workflows.
skill.summary: Build ChatGPT Apps with interactive widgets and MCP server templates.
skill.author: Asmayaseen

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, environment, and blocker patterns, but most are markdown code examples, inline code terms, or placeholder URLs. No prompt injection attempt, malicious credential collection, or exfiltration behavior was found. Residual risk comes from development guidance that exposes a local MCP server through ngrok with no auth examples and reset commands that kill local processes.

medium_findings.title: Public Development Tunnel and No-Auth Example
medium_findings.description: The templates instruct users to run a FastMCP server on host 0.0.0.0 and connect it through an ngrok URL. They also show No Auth for development, which is legitimate for local testing but risky if copied into production.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines clearly show a public tunnel workflow and no-auth registration guidance. The context is development documentation, so the risk is real but not malicious.

medium_findings.title: Process-Killing Reset Commands
medium_findings.description: The debugging guides include reset commands using lsof, xargs kill -9, and pkill ngrok. These are troubleshooting commands, but they can terminate unrelated local processes if reused without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and potentially disruptive. They are scoped to development reset workflows, which lowers confidence that this is a security threat.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Hits Are Mostly Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: The Ruby backtick detections are false positives from markdown fences and inline command examples. The skill does not contain automation that executes these snippets by itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation blocks or code examples, not executable skill hooks. No evidence found of hidden shell execution logic.

low_findings.title: Environment File References Are Template Guidance
low_findings.description: The .env and python-dotenv references appear in a sample project structure and dependency list. No evidence found that the skill reads or transmits environment secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is limited to scaffolding text and dependency guidance. There is no matching network code that sends environment values elsewhere.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Localhost and Placeholder Documentation
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings point to localhost, ChatGPT setup pages, and example ngrok domains. These are expected for ChatGPT Apps development and do not indicate data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible development endpoints and placeholders. No evidence found of a covert or attacker-controlled collection endpoint.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Blocker Hits Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography labels map to normal ChatGPT Apps text, metadata examples, or placeholder content. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or other weak cryptographic operations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of representative flagged lines found documentation text, not cryptographic code. The high static severity is not supported by semantic context.

dangerous_patterns.title: No-Auth Public Tunnel Development Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill demonstrates exposing a local server through ngrok and registering it with No Auth. Users should restrict this to development and add authentication for shared or production apps.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The lines directly describe the tunnel and no-auth setup. The surrounding text frames it as development setup rather than malicious behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Force-Kill Troubleshooting Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The reset workflow force-kills processes on port 8001 and terminates ngrok. This can disrupt local development if another service is using the same port.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is explicit and potentially destructive to local processes. It is still a normal reset pattern for a developer troubleshooting guide.

content.value_statement: Building ChatGPT Apps with widgets requires precise MCP metadata, UI structure, and Developer Mode setup. This skill provides templates, patterns, and debugging steps for interactive OpenAI Apps SDK projects.
actual_capabilities: Explains ChatGPT App architecture with MCP servers and embedded widgets.
Provides FastMCP server templates with widget resource metadata.
Shows widget communication patterns using window.openai APIs.
Documents response metadata for text/html+skybridge widgets.
Gives Developer Mode setup steps with ngrok for local testing.
Includes debugging guidance for caching, rendering, and tool discovery issues.
limitations: Focused on ChatGPT Apps with widgets, not generic MCP server design.
Examples use development tunnels and no-auth setup that need hardening for production.
Widget behavior must be verified inside ChatGPT because browser previews lack window.openai.
Some guidance may require updates as the OpenAI Apps SDK changes.

use_cases.title: Prototype a Widget App
use_cases.description: Create a local FastMCP app that renders a simple interactive widget inside ChatGPT.

use_cases.title: Debug Widget Rendering
use_cases.description: Troubleshoot cached widgets, missing metadata, MIME type errors, and tool discovery problems.

use_cases.title: Design App Interaction Patterns
use_cases.description: Choose reliable widget actions for follow-up prompts, structured data return, and tool chaining.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Starter App
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to create a minimal ChatGPT App with one FastMCP tool and one embedded widget.

prompt_templates.title: Add Widget Actions
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to add reliable widget buttons that send follow-up messages and return selected data.

prompt_templates.title: Fix Rendering Problems
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to diagnose why my ChatGPT App widget is stuck loading or not updating.

prompt_templates.title: Review App Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review my ChatGPT App structure, metadata, widget registry, and development tunnel setup.

output_examples.input: I need a first ChatGPT App that shows a small interactive widget.
output_examples.output: A project outline, FastMCP server plan, widget resource plan, and setup checklist for Developer Mode testing.

output_examples.input: My widget stays on the loading screen after the tool runs.
output: Check that the tool response includes widget metadata.
Confirm the MIME type is text/html+skybridge.
Recreate the app if ChatGPT is showing a cached widget.

output_examples.input: I want buttons inside the widget to continue the conversation.
output_examples.output: A recommended action-button pattern using follow-up messages, with notes about ChatGPT iframe limitations.

best_practices: Keep widget HTML, CSS, and JavaScript self-contained for reliable ChatGPT rendering.
Treat ngrok and no-auth setup as development-only, then add auth before broader use.
Test widgets inside ChatGPT because the window.openai bridge is not available in normal browser previews.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill for standard MCP servers that do not render widgets.
Do not assume complex browser APIs or chained click handlers will work inside the ChatGPT iframe.
Do not publish a public tunnel or no-auth endpoint without reviewing access controls.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps build ChatGPT Apps that combine MCP tools with embedded interactive widgets.

faq.question: Does it support Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The marketplace metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: Is this for normal MCP servers?
faq.answer: No. It is best for ChatGPT Apps that need widgets and OpenAI Apps SDK metadata.

faq.question: Why does it mention ngrok?
faq.answer: ChatGPT needs a reachable MCP endpoint during local development, so the guides show ngrok tunnel setup.

faq.question: What are the main security concerns?
faq.answer: The main concerns are public development tunnels, no-auth examples, and reset commands that kill local processes.

faq.question: Can I use the examples in production?
faq.answer: Use them as starting points only. Add authentication, review exposed endpoints, and update SDK details before production use.
